title: Managing sheep in winter housing
subtitle: Space, bedding, feeding, and daily checks for late-pregnancy ewes housed through the cold months.
excerpt: Housing late-pregnancy ewes protects them from cold, wet, and poaching on winter pasture. But a badly managed shed creates its own problems: respiratory disease, footrot, and pregnancy toxaemia all increase when ewes are crowded or underfed. This tutorial covers the practical requirements for a safe indoor system from housing to turnout.
sourceNotes:
AHDB Beef and Lamb, Better Returns Programme: Winter Housing of Sheep (2013). https://ahdb.org.uk
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Sheep (1998). https://www.gov.uk
NADAIS Pregnancy Toxaemia Guidance. https://www.nadis.org.uk

glossaryTerms.definition: Also called twin lamb disease. A metabolic condition in late-pregnancy ewes carrying multiple lambs. It occurs when the energy demands of the growing lambs exceed the ewe's dietary intake. The ewe mobilises her own fat reserves faster than her liver can process them. Symptoms include lethargy, head pressing, and reluctance to move. Prompt treatment with a glucose drench or propylene glycol can recover mild cases; severe cases are often fatal.

A high-energy, high-protein compound feed fed in addition to forage (hay or silage) to meet the increased nutritional demands of late pregnancy and lactation. Typically fed as pellets or nuts at 200 to 500 g per head per day depending on litter size and condition score. Introduces too fast or in too-large portions causes acidosis; build up from nil over two to three weeks.

A manual assessment of the fat cover on a ewe by feeling the backbone and ribs. Scored from 1 (very thin) to 5 (obese). The ideal score at housing for a late-pregnancy ewe is 3 to 3.5. Below 2, the ewe is at high risk of pregnancy toxaemia. Above 4, she is at risk of complications at lambing.

content.text: Not every flock needs housing. Hardy hill breeds in good condition often do better outside through mild winters. The decision to house is usually about the land: late-pregnancy ewes on wet clay will poach a field to mud in days. Once the pasture is gone, housing is cheaper than reseeding. Whatever the reason for bringing them in, the management principles are the same.
Allow 1.2 square metres of lying space per ewe as a minimum. Ewes carrying twins or triplets, or those with a

below 3, need more room and should not be at the bottom of the pecking order. Pen heavily pregnant ewes separately from younger or lighter animals. Allow at least 300 mm of trough space per ewe at the hay rack so every animal can eat at the same time.

Wheat straw is the standard bedding material. Start with at least 15 cm on a concrete floor. Add fresh straw daily to the wettest areas, particularly around water troughs. Check the total depth every three to four days: once it falls below 10 cm, ewes are lying on hard cold flooring. A full muck-out mid-housing is worthwhile if the bedding becomes compacted and damp.

Forage should be available ad lib for the first eight weeks of a 16-week housing period. In the final six to eight weeks of pregnancy, energy requirements increase sharply, particularly in ewes carrying multiples. This is when to introduce

. Start at 100 g per head per day and increase gradually to 400 to 500 g for twin-bearing ewes in the last month. Silage of unknown dry matter content can cause inconsistent intake; if you are using silage, have it analysed so you can adjust the concentrate ration accordingly.

attrs.title: Pregnancy toxaemia risk
attrs.body: Pregnancy toxaemia kills ewes, particularly those carrying two or more lambs. Any ewe that is off her feed, standing apart from the group, or showing signs of depression in the last month of pregnancy should be treated promptly with an energy drench. Call your vet if she does not respond within 24 hours.

content.text: are those carrying multiples, those with a condition score below 2.5, and those that have been off feed for any reason during the last six weeks of pregnancy. Scan results (if you have them) allow you to separate twin and triplet-bearing ewes and feed them more heavily than singles.
Check that every animal gets up easily and moves without a limp.
Check hay racks and water supplies are full and accessible to all animals.
Walk through the pen and look for any ewe that is standing alone or looks dull.
In the last three weeks before lambing, look for udder development and any ewe showing signs of imminent labour.
